speaker: interviewer
text: --in Rancho Mirage, California. Renee Firestone is the interviewer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were liberated, and you were in a hospital. Whose hospital was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was not really a hospital. It was in one of the buildings of the original German Army camp that has been outside of the little town of Bergen for many, many years. The British Army, who was the main army that liberated that part of Germany with the Canadians-- although it was mostly British Army-- I don't know which army it was. The Second Army, it could have been. They took over the German Army base camp and the room-- a large room-- that had been turned into the hospital facility. What I mean by that, there were all iron bedsteads, and I was in one of those beds, as was my sister and the other children of our [? diamond ?] group. I don't remember how long I was in the hospital bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maurice, I found it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how long I was in the hospital bed. But I remember a lady came to my-- a nurse came to my bedside. And I spoke a few words of English, not many, although she was a Dutch lady. And I always remembered and to this day, her name. Her name, her last name-- I don't remember her first name-- was Jahemstahl. Jahemstahl the family Jahemstahl-- it's a German name-- and before the war, I knew that the family Jahemstahl had been in Holland in a publishing business. And she must have been related to that family. Because my mother had taught my sister and I to memorize my grandmother's address in England, in London, I asked her-- this Dutch lady, Jewish Dutch lady of German descent with that name who was connected to the Red Cross with the British Army-- I asked her if she could find my grandmother, get in touch with my grandmother in England. And I told her the address, which I had memorized. And to this day, of course, I know that. It is Number 2 Devon Rise, East Finchley, London Northwest, I think it was. I remember being for some time in that hospital and then being out of the hospital. Although before I was out, she-- a person-- a person came to my bed in the hospital. I don't remember exactly, although I know the date. And they came to tell me that my sister had died while in the hospital. I remember, at that time, crying about that. When I was better, or when I was let out of the hospital-- and I don't remember where I slept when I was out of the hospital-- one of the children that was with me as part of the [? transport ?] she was older than me. She was about 15 at that time when I was still 13. Her name was Hetty and her last name is Werkendum. And she has two brothers. She had two brothers with her in the camp-- one called Jackie and one called Max, or he called himself Max. And I remember her very much. She always stood out in my mind when we were in the barrack with Luba and indeed, when she came to me, and she took me to the place where my sister was buried. At that time, or rather soon after that time, I, in a way, tried to erase that out of my mind. I remember, thereafter, that one day we all got into army vehicles-- all us children who were no longer in the hospital-- and went to look at the last guard tower of Belsen being set alight by an army flamethrower. And I remember being there with a lot of other people-- i.e. Inmates of the camp-- watching and all cheering when this thing went up in flames. Soon thereafter, and it was in June, there appeared within us, with us, a Dutch Jewish man who was also in the, I would say, group which was called Vrij Nederland. There was a brigade in the British Army-- or connected to the British Army-- called Vrij Nederland, which is Free Holland. Those were Dutch people who had gotten to England and had joined up. He came to us and arranged for us to be taken out of Germany. And we were driven to a small airport, airfield, in a lorry, truck, and we were put on an airplane. It was the first time after the war that I was in an airplane, and it was a Army Dakota. I remember it so very, very well because it had square windows. And in the window was a rubber plug. And when I asked about it, it was so that the soldiers who were being transported in that plane, they could defend the plane by sticking their rifles through the plug, through the hole. We were flown, in June '45, two months after the liberation of the camp, approximately two months. We were flown to a town in the south of Holland called Eindhoven. Eindhoven happens to be the headquarters of the very well-known Philips Company. We were received there in a-- like an army-- not an army base, a facility where they had taken over a local school. And in the schoolrooms, they had made like dormitories. And we arrived there. And when we arrived there, a uniformed Dutch person-- in uniform same as the British uniform, khaki uniform-- asked us questions about our name, our father's name, mother's name, the last address we were at in Holland, which camp we came from. And that form, that card, I've kept all my life. And a few years ago, I donated the original to the Washington Holocaust Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we for a moment go back to Bergen-Belsen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Just before liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what happened to the Germans, to the Nazis? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in the camp-- after the liberation-- every day before we were taken out of the camp to go to the hospital, the Germans and Hungarians-- there were Hungarian soldiers there who had been deprived of their weapons by the Allies, as indeed were the Germans deprived of their weapons. They were all commanded to move the bodies-- to take the bodies, pick them up, throw them into trucks and into wagons to be transported to the mass graves. And while I was still in the camp, I used to see them just with bulldozers. Just after the mass grave was dug there, the bulldozers would just bulldoze all the people into the graves. And where the bulldozers couldn't come because of the narrowness between the barracks where the people-- the dead bodies, the corpses-- were laying, the little truck would come, or the trolley would come. And the Germans, under guard of British soldiers with weapons-- either Tommy guns or rifles-- the Germans would have to load the corpses onto those vehicles for them to be taken to the mass grave. This happened virtually day and night in the camp after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember liberation itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: A great amount of joy amongst us children and the adults that were still able to walk. The ones that could not walk, sitting down, couldn't get up, laying in the dirt, couldn't move, too sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at the time exactly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was outside the barrack with the other children, with Luba, going towards-- being amongst the great amount of people. I never climbed on any vehicle or anything like that. As far as I can remember, a great aura of relief and happiness and thankfulness to something that we were liberated. I remember the soldiers giving sweets to us. Chewing gum was a-- I remember Wrigley's chewing gum. Cigarettes I saw given to grown-ups. I wasn't in the-- in years, grown up at that time, just by experience grown up. There was-- food came, food which Luba doled out, as I said before. Most of the food, of course, was army rations, because an army that is fighting is not the army that stayed there. They left, and another army came. And then the doctors and the Red Cross people came. But in the beginning, it was just the fighting forces that came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did Luba leave? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: INT2: Well, as I said before, when I went into the hospital, after they deloused us, I never saw Luba again. As far as I knew, at that time, Luba stayed there. I-- I neglected to say that at that time, I remember them telling me I weighed 60 pounds. That was my-- that was my body weight. And I was 13 years, one month at the liberation, 15th of April 1945. Yes, I-- I didn't know at that time what happened to Luba. And I was not to know for very, very many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of your birthday, the  13th birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was aware of my 13th birthday. I was aware the fact that no bar mitzvah. I was aware of the fact that maybe I didn't want to be bar mitzvahed. I was of aware of the fact-- as I have been many different occasions throughout my life-- that it's not such a great thing to be Jewish, that it's not such a-- maybe such a mitzvah. As all my life-- more so when I never came to the United States-- before I came to the United States, I've always been rather reluctant to say I'm Jewish because my feelings about it is-- because of being Jewish, terrible things have happened to me and my family. And I'm convinced today, cynical though it may seem, that there is almost no less anti-Semitism now as what was then. And I fear it. I fear it not for me, obviously. I fear it for my fellow Jews all over the world, including in Israel. But I fear it also because of, I suppose, the old kind of thought of what happens to the Jews will eventually happen to others, which indeed is how things happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that you showed me a picture of your sister in bed in Bergen-Belsen. How did that picture come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken by the part of the army-- British Army-- that liberated Belsen, that came into the barrack. They took the picture of my sister in her bunk bed at time of the liberation. And that picture eventually wound up in the Imperial War Museum in London in England. And we got a copy of that picture from the Imperial War Museum in England. It was taken either on the liberation day on the 15th of April, or it was taken very soon thereafter, because the date of my sister's death is the 24th of April, 1945, which is exactly nine days after the liberation that she died in the hospital outside Bergen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us that your-- this 15-year-old girl--

speaker: interviewee
text: 12. My sister was 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, the girl that was 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, Hetty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hetty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That she took you to her grave site.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she know where the grave was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she was the eldest amongst us. And she, by her nature, I think, is a person that gets organized and busy. She must have asked. Or maybe she was, at the time, with the people that buried my sister. I don't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also told me that this girl did a-- had a great impression on you throughout the imprisonment and after.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the reason being she was the eldest. I think she helped me with my sister. If I wanted to know something or help, I would go to her because I could speak to her in Dutch, whereas I couldn't speak Polish to Luba. I could speak a few words German, but very few. My best language was Dutch. And I would speak to Hetty. Hetty would speak to Luba or whoever. And funnily enough, looking back, thinking about it, I think maybe that was the first knowledge I had of having some sexual attraction to a member of the opposite sex because she was very pretty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's go back to the barracks to where you were in the hospital for a while. And then they took you back to Holland. What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland, we were in the city of Eindhoven. We slept on the floor after giving our details of who we were, which were filled out on white cards. I don't know how long I was in Eindhoven, but it certainly was more than one night. It may have been a week. After that time, we were all put into buses-- buses of that era-- and driven to Amsterdam. Excuse me. And I remember looking through the windows and seeing all the destruction in Amsterdam, around Nijmegen where the big paratroop drop was for the British, and different towns and saw a lot of destruction. Although the destruction in Holland was not so much from bombing. It was more from actual fighting to liberate the country. We were driven to Amsterdam. And we were driven to a building in Amsterdam, which was the building which housed-- before the war, the union of [? diamond ?] people had their own building. And that building-- I believe it is still there. I haven't seen it since the time. We were taken to that building, all us children that had gotten out of Germany. And in that building, we were received by Dutch Jewish adults. And they asked each one who we were. And I told them who I was, and they told me [PAUSES] that my mother was in Sweden, [PAUSES] that-- that she was alive in Sweden. It was the first time since the beginning of 19-- December '44 that I knew my mother was alive. And they asked me if I had anywhere to go. I said the only people I know, I can go to, is my friend Jopi.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the only clothes I had was this pair of shorts they had given me in a-- in a-- the hospital after I came out of bed. They gave me pair of lederhosen. And I had a-- like a pillowcase with a bit of string, drawstring, and there's a few clothes in there. That's what I had. And I told them where my friend Jopi lived because we'd be neighbors, I knew. And then the same day, maybe later in the day, his father-- Jopi's father-- came to collect me. And he took me home to their house. [PAUSES] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me that he-- that he had a letter from my uncle in England through that woman's efforts from the Red Cross, and that my uncle was trying to get me permission from the British government to get me to England. I stayed with Jopi for some time. I don't know or remember how long exactly. I-- I could, I suppose, look through some of my papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew that my mother was alive in Sweden, as they had told me. But I knew-- or I didn't know whether anybody else was alive. So as far as I knew, at that moment in time, I was alive. And I knew my mother was in Sweden, and that's all I knew. And I knew I had my family-- my mother's family-- in England. And all I wanted to do is get to England to my grandmother and my great-grandmother and my uncles and aunts.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Holland I forget how long, not all that long. And then Jopi's father took me to Hook of Holland. The Hook of Holland is a harbor outside of Rotterdam, where I used to go from as a child to England. Now he took me there to put me on the boat with another man, and the other man was to look after me. And I went in the same clothes I had that they'd given me in the camp. And those days, the boat journey would have taken about six, seven hours. And my-- the boat arrived in London itself, in Gravesend, which is a part on the Thames in London. I arrived there, and my uncle was there-- my mother's brother, my mother's eldest brother, who was in the Royal Navy, the British Navy. And he was going to take me to my grandmother. And then he told me that my grandmother was dead. As she had been told, there was news about the camps in the newspaper. So she went to buy the newspaper and stepped off the sidewalk and got hit by a [INAUDIBLE] truck and died.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the house where my mother's other sister was, her sister Lily, and my great-grandmother, Granny Klock, and my mother's-- mother's sister, in other words, my mother's Aunt [? Rae, ?] Rachel. And her name was Italeander, which is Dutch for Italian, because her husband's parents had come from Italy. So I arrived at my-- thinking my grandmother's house. There was my Granny Klock there, my great-grandmother, my mother's other sister Lily, her husband and her two children, and my Auntie [? Rae ?], who was a widow. So that was a happy/sad occasion for me to arrive back there alone to England, where I had come and I'd been with my sister as a child on so many occasions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always had terribly happy memories for me, places where my aunts and uncles always bought me presents, and my grandmother always bought me clothes, English clothes, where with-- which were then good-- the best you could get in the world. So it was a place of happy memories for me on this terrible, sad time when I got there, terrible. As far as I knew then, and know now, my mother was still in Sweden. I didn't know what happened to my Auntie Annie or my cousin John at that time. [SIGHS] My uncle, my uncle, my-- Sam, my mother's eldest brother, thought I should go to school because I hadn't been really to proper school for many years. And his son, whose name is the same as mine, he was going to a private school, and my uncle thought that maybe he should send me to the same school. Well, I went for two terms to this school in the Hampstead Garden Suburb in London-- it's a well-known area in London-- with a very little English. And I was starting from-- for my age-- way behind the other boys. But I suppose I'm a quick learner in some respects. Although the boys, not all Jewish boys at that school, but all English boys at that school, started making fun of me very much because of my inability to speak English. And when I did speak English, it was always with a terrible accent. It-- it caused me a feeling of less than good, less than the same. And in many respects, that less than the same, or less than equal to, has stuck with me throughout my life. I've never been able to shake that from me. And yet I know that, in many respects, I'm more than. But I have this terrible, terrible sense of less than. [SIGHS] I then-- when I came out and went to school, I was very much anti-authority. I couldn't stand being told what to do or where to go or nothing. Nobody could tell me nothing. I knew what they had not the faintest idea of what I knew. I, at that time, even had differences of opinion-- I don't want to call it arguments-- with my uncle, who kitted me out with all new clothes and threw away what I'd got from the camp-- a school uniform, as is the custom in England, to have uniform at school. But I could not be told by no master in class, by no uncle, or anyone which way was up.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to ask you whether your mother knew that you were alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether my mother knew whether I was alive. What I did know that after I didn't want to remain at school, I wanted to go back to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We have to change tapes.
